theme: highway sensor loops before the jam
title: Highway Sensor Loops Before the Jam — recovering the hidden conserved flux
strategies: trivial: submit the checker's own zero-flux baseline f(rho)=0 (no traffic ever moves) -> reproduces the baseline exactly, Ratio ~0.1

greedy: treat the calm-day snapshots as a rigidly translating pattern; cross-correlate consecutive profiles to find one bulk shift speed c and submit the density-independent flux f(rho)=c*rho. Fits the smooth training data reasonably but has no way to make characteristics converge, so it cannot predict where or how fast the held-out steep initial condition breaks into a shockwave

strong: recognise the CONSERVED quantity's own structure -- rearrange rho_t=-(f(rho))_x=-f'(rho)*rho_x into a LINEAR least-squares problem for the coefficients of f'(rho) (a low-degree polynomial in rho) using finite-difference estimates of rho_t and rho_x from the sensor grid, then integrate to recover f(rho) itself. This exploits characteristics visible even in smooth data to extrapolate the correct concave shape (and hence the correct Rankine-Hugoniot shock speed) to the unseen high-density regime

headroom above strong: better-conditioned finite differences (e.g. total-variation-regularised or higher-order stencils), a wider polynomial family, or explicit noise-aware weighting of the conservation-law regression could push held-out MAE further toward the numerical-scheme's own discretization floor
